glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons) as described in the text.
glossary.sentence: Connectionist artificial intelligence System that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons) An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems.

glossary.definition: An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems as described in the text.
glossary.sentence: Symbolic artificial intelligence An artificial intelligence system that mimics the functioning of human brain (large number of interconnected neurons) An artificial intelligence system based on symbolic (human-readable) representations of problems.

glossary.definition: Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence, that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc. as described in the text.
glossary.sentence: Narrow (weak) artificial intelligence , that is also called immature or limited artificial intelligence, is based on the view that intelligence is a set of abilities and is therefore concerned with developing solutions for some specific tasks such as chess playing, medical diagnosis, etc.

glossary.definition: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) . It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) . Thus, this approach attempts to achieve a level of intelligent performance of an intelligent computer system where the system is able to solve a wide range of tasks, including novel tasks, and perform them as well as a human (Russell and Norvig, 2020) .
glossary.sentence: Artificial general intelligence or, in another words, strong artificial intelligence, which is also called human-level intelligence (Russell and Norvig, 2020) , is an approach treats intelligence as a general capability that can be copied/replicated (Callan, 2003) . It is related to the development of such computer systems that are capable of solving a wide range of tasks in different problem domains and controlling themselves autonomously with their own thoughts, worries, feelings, strengths, weaknesses and predispositions ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) .

glossary.definition: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) .
glossary.sentence: Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) .

glossary.definition: 'It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales' as described in the text.
glossary.sentence: ‘It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales’ ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008). Practitioners of new artificial intelligence assert that true intelligence involves the ability to function in a real-world environment (Copeland, 2018; Pfeifer and Scheier, 2001) . Brooks (1990) , the founder of this approach, has emphasised that the new artificial intelligence is based on the hypothesis that in order to build an intelligent system, it is necessary that their representations are grounded in the physical world, since the world is the best model of itself; in this case, of course, it is necessary to equip the system with sensors in order for the system to perceive the environment and actuators in order for the system to be able to operate in the environment.

glossary.definition: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents. It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) . These two sub-directions are used in search and optimisation.
glossary.sentence: Swarm intelligence is a paradigm under new artificial intelligence. It considers collective intelligence as a behaviour that emerges through the interaction and cooperation of large numbers of lesser intelligent agents. It includes ant colony optimization , which studies probabilistic algorithms, inspired by ant behaviour when they are searching for food, and particle swarm optimization that studies probabilistic algorithms inspired by birds and fish (Brownlee, 2011) .

glossary.definition: Weak methods use general methods (like general purpose search, logics, syntax-based calculations, automatic reasoning, etc.) which could be applied to a broad set of problems. They do not use knowledge on a problem under consideration; strong methods are based on the great amount of knowledge about a problem under consideration and, thus, they take into account problem solving context; strong methods typically use weak methods.
glossary.sentence: Weak methods use general methods (like general purpose search, logics, syntax-based calculations, automatic reasoning, etc.) which could be applied to a broad set of problems. They do not use knowledge on a problem under consideration; strong methods are based on the great amount of knowledge about a problem under consideration and, thus, they take into account problem solving context; strong methods typically use weak methods.

glossary.definition: Strong methods are based on the great amount of knowledge about a problem under consideration and, thus, they take into account problem solving context; strong methods typically use weak methods.
glossary.sentence: Strong methods are based on the great amount of knowledge about a problem under consideration and, thus, they take into account problem solving context; strong methods typically use weak methods.

glossary.definition: The following necessary characteristics are defined for artificial general intelligence ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) : the ability to solve general problems in a non-domain-restricted way, in the same sense that a human can; the ability to solve problems in particular domains and particular contexts with particular efficiency; the ability to use its more generalized and more specialized intelligence capabilities together, in a unified way; the ability to learn from its environment, other intelligent systems, and teachers (those, who teach such a kind of systems ); the ability to become better at solving novel types of problems as it gains experience with them. Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) . Additionally, to the previously mentioned approaches there is also the so called new/nouvelle artificial intelligence . 'It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales' ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008). Practitioners of new artificial intelligence assert that true intelligence involves the ability to function in a real-world environment (Copeland, 2018; Pfeifer and Scheier, 2001) . Brooks (1990) , the founder of this approach, has emphasised that the new artificial intelligence is based on the hypothesis that in order to build an intelligent system, it is necessary that their representations are grounded in the physical world, since the world is the best model of itself; in this case, of course, it is necessary to equip the system with sensors in order for the system to perceive the environment and actuators in order for the system to be able to operate in the environment.
glossary.sentence: The following necessary characteristics are defined for artificial general intelligence ( Pennachin and Goertzel , 2007) : the ability to solve general problems in a non-domain-restricted way, in the same sense that a human can; the ability to solve problems in particular domains and particular contexts with particular efficiency; the ability to use its more generalized and more specialized intelligence capabilities together, in a unified way; the ability to learn from its environment, other intelligent systems, and teachers (those, who teach such a kind of systems ); the ability to become better at solving novel types of problems as it gains experience with them. Artificial super intelligence is an intelligence that surpasses human capabilities (Russell and Norvig, 2020) and therefore it is much smarter than the best human minds in practically every field, including scientific creativity, general wisdom and social skills (Bostrom, 2015) . Additionally, to the previously mentioned approaches there is also the so called new/nouvelle artificial intelligence . 'It focuses on a wide range of organisms, processes, and phenomena that occur at multiple spatial and temporal scales' ( Floreano and Mattiussi, 2008). Practitioners of new artificial intelligence assert that true intelligence involves the ability to function in a real-world environment (Copeland, 2018; Pfeifer and Scheier, 2001) . Brooks (1990) , the founder of this approach, has emphasised that the new artificial intelligence is based on the hypothesis that in order to build an intelligent system, it is necessary that their representations are grounded in the physical world, since the world is the best model of itself; in this case, of course, it is necessary to equip the system with sensors in order for the system to perceive the environment and actuators in order for the system to be able to operate in the environment.

glossary.definition: A state space graph is a representation of all possible states in a game, where each state can be represented as a node and transitions between states are defined by rules.
glossary.sentence: The state space graph that you can see in Figure 9. ... In the case of a complete state space graph, the graph can be created and stored in a data storage (such as a file, database, etc.) during the design and implementation stages of the intelligent computer system, or the graph can be generated during the operation of the intelligent computer system, if the state-transition rules of the game can easily be formalised.

glossary.definition: A root node in the state space graph that represents the starting point of a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: The robot starts at location C1, which we can represent as a root node in the state space graph. The initial state(s) of a problem-solving process are contained in IS.

glossary.definition: A leaf node in the state space graph that represents the end of a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: The goal state(s) of a problem-solving process are displayed by leaf nodes of the state space graph.

glossary.definition: An action is represented by an arc in the state space graph, which connects two states (nodes). These correspond to steps/actions in a problem-solving process.
glossary.sentence: In the state space graph actions are represented by arcs, and together with intermediate states they define a course of problem-solving. ... Actions are represented by arcs, and together with intermediate states they define a course of problem-solving.

glossary.definition: A specific type of state space graph used in the context of this study course to demonstrate search-based problem solving applied to game playing.
glossary.sentence: In this course, to demonstrate the application of search-based problem solving to the representation and implementation of game playing, we will focus on a relatively simple type of game: zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information. ... This category of games includes both simple games such as tic-tac-toe and relatively complex ones such as chess, checkers, and Go.

glossary.term: zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information
glossary.definition: A type of game where two players compete against each other. The game is characterized by the following properties: (1) There are two players who take turns making moves; (2) both players have complete access to all information about the course of the game, including past and future moves; (3) there are no hidden factors affecting the outcome of the game, such as random elements or unknowns. This type of game is also known as a zero-sum game.
glossary.sentence: This category of games includes both simple games such as tic-tac-toe and relatively complex ones such as chess, checkers, and Go.

glossary.definition: A type of game where all players have complete knowledge about the current state of the game. In this context, it means that each player can see the entire board or know the exact state of the game in advance.
glossary.sentence: In the context of our course, these could be a human and an intelligent computer system, or two intelligent computer systems playing against each other; both players make alternate moves until the end of the game. ... Both players have access to complete information about the course of the game (including past moves, future moves, and the available objects used in the game).

glossary.definition: A type of game where some of the information about the current state is hidden from the players. In this context, it means that each player can only see their own cards (in the case of bridge) or some other objects (e.g., in poker), and they cannot know what the other players are doing.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic multi-player games of imperfect information, such as bridge and poker, in which part of the information about the course of the game is hidden from the players (i.e. the players can see only their own cards) and where a die could also be used to introduce randomness.

glossary.definition: A type of game that involves some level of randomness, such as when rolling dice in backgammon or using a random number generator in simulations. In this context, it means that the players cannot make precise predictions about the outcome of their actions.
glossary.sentence: Stochastic two-player games of perfect information, such as backgammon, in which both players still are aware of what is happening on the playing board but a die is used, meaning that the players cannot make a precise prediction about the score on the die in the next move. ... Stochastic multi-player games of imperfect information, such as bridge and poker, in which part of the information about the course of the game is hidden from the players (i.e. the players can see only their own cards) and where a die could also be used to introduce randomness.

glossary.definition: A method for solving problems using search algorithms, which involves exploring different possibilities in a structured manner to find a solution.
glossary.sentence: In this course, to demonstrate the application of search-based problem solving to the representation and implementation of game playing, we will focus on a relatively simple type of game: zero-sum deterministic two-player games with perfect information. ... To eliminate the discrepancy between these states, we perform actions and pass through a series of intermediate states (Figure 2).

glossary.definition: The search start node is the initial node in the algorithm's processing, which is typically the root node of the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The search start node is A. The search goal node is K. ... The search start node is added to the OPEN list together with its heuristic measure.

glossary.definition: The search goal node is the target node that the algorithm aims to find during the search process.
glossary.sentence: The search goal node is K. The algorithm stops when the search goal node is added to the OPEN list or when leaf nodes with heuristic measures better than the heuristic measure of the search goal node are reached.

glossary.definition: An ordered sequence of actions that lead to the solution to the problem.
glossary.sentence: It corresponds to an ordered sequence of actions that lead to the solution to the problem.

glossary.definition: Nodes whose descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated.
glossary.sentence: Open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated.

glossary.definition: Nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.
glossary.sentence: Closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.

glossary.definition: A method used to solve problems by exploring a state space graph. The search algorithm sequentially explores different paths in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: The search principles explained previously have shown that while searching for a solution path, the search algorithm sequentially explores different paths in the state space graph. This process may result in several situations that determine how fast and whether the search algorithm will find a solution path.

glossary.definition: Forward chaining is a technique for gathering information and then inferring from it whatever can be inferred (Negnevitsky , 2011). It is typically used for problems of interpretation because, in this type of problems, most of the data are initially given, and the system searches for their interpretation (Luger, 2009; Hopgood, 2001).
glossary.sentence: Forward chaining is a technique...

glossary.definition: In goal-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory; the control cycle 'Recognize-act' matches the pattern against the THEN part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its IF part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when facts about the problem are found.
glossary.sentence: In goal-driven search...

glossary.definition: In data-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): the search begins with a problem description or given data about the problem (a pattern) that are added to the working memory; the control cycle 'Recognize-act' matches the pattern against the IF part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its THEN part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when the search goal is found.
glossary.sentence: In data-driven search...

glossary.definition: In goal-driven search (Luger, 2005; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992): a goal (a pattern) is added to the working memory; the control cycle 'Recognize-act' matches the pattern against the THEN part of production rules in the knowledge base; firing a rule, its IF part is added to the working memory, and the process continues; searching stops when facts about the problem are found.
glossary.sentence: In goal-driven search...

glossary.definition: The control cycle performs the following steps: the working memory contains a pattern corresponding to the current state of the problem-solving process; the pattern is matched against the IF or THEN part (it depends on the search direction) of all rules available in the knowledge base; if several rules match the pattern, a conflict set is acquired.
glossary.sentence: The control cycle performs the following steps...

glossary.definition: The main steps involved in the process of finding a solution path through the state space graph: selecting the current node, checking if it is the goal node, expanding the node by generating descendants/ancestors, and determining whether a solution has been found.
glossary.sentence: The search algorithm steps (Figure 5): Selecting the current node. Checking if the current node is the search goal node. If the search goal node is found, then it means that a solution path is generated and search stops. Otherwise, the third step is performed. Expanding the current node by generating its descendants/ancestors (depending on a search direction). As a result of this action, two types of nodes appear: open nodes – the search algorithm has already generated these nodes, but their descendants/ancestors have not yet been generated; closed nodes – they are nodes whose descendants/ancestors have already been generated.

glossary.definition: Backtracking is a meta-strategy for systematically trying different paths through a state space graph, because its ideas are used by other search algorithms that are able to find a solution path in a more effective way than Backtracking. The idea is very simple – if the search goal node is not reached by the current path, then it is necessary to backtrack to the most recent node on the path having unexamined descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction) and to try another path.
glossary.sentence: Backtracking introduces several practical aspects related to the implementation of other search algorithms (adopted from (Luger, 2009)) : a list of nodes awaiting expansion is needed to support backtracking; a list of nodes whose descendants/ancestors (depending on a search direction) have not led to the search goal node should be kept; such a list will allow avoiding cycles and consideration the same node many times; it is necessary to keep information about the current path being tried; if the search goal node is found, then the solution path can be reconstructed.

glossary.definition: The target node in the state space graph that the search algorithm aims to reach.
glossary.sentence: The search goal node is M. The search algorithm will first generate the path A->B->F->J. It will determine that this path does not lead to the search goal node, and the search algorithm will continue expanding nodes and will generate the path A->B->G->K.

glossary.definition: search algorithms that do not use heuristic information to evaluate node quality. They process only nodes and arcs of the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Uninformed search algorithms rarely include other steps. Therefore, uninformed search algorithms receive the search start node and the search goal node as input data (Figure 3). They use the state space graph as a data structure to search for a problem solution.

glossary.definition: search algorithms that use heuristic information to evaluate node quality. These factors are represented as numerical values showing the quality of each node in the state space graph.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic search algorithms are able to distinguish between paths represented in the state space graph. Therefore, each path differs from all of the others.

glossary.definition: Depth-First Search is a search algorithm that explores the state space graph by expanding nodes in a depth-first manner. It expands the current node I by generating its three descendants - nodes J, K, and G. The algorithm checks if it is the search goal node. It is the search goal node. Therefore, the algorithm stops its operation.
glossary.sentence: Depth-First Search is a search algorithm that explores the state space graph by expanding nodes in a depth-first manner. It expands the current node I by generating its three descendants - nodes J, K, and G. The algorithm checks if it is the search goal node. It is the search goal node. Therefore, the algorithm stops its operation.

glossary.definition: The OPEN list is a list that contains nodes that are currently being considered for processing in the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm takes the node located on the first position in the OPEN list—the node A—and it becomes the current node. The algorithm checks if it is the search goal node. It is not the search goal node; thus, the algorithm starts the next iteration.

glossary.definition: The CLOSED list is a list that contains nodes that have been expanded and are no longer considered for further processing.
glossary.sentence: The CLOSED list is empty because none of the available nodes has been expanded so far. The node A is moved to the CLOSED list as an already expanded node. The nodes E and H are moved to the CLOSED list as already expanded nodes.

glossary.definition: a data structure that works according to the 'Last in, First Out' principle: the last data element entered is the first to be processed.
glossary.sentence: In the implementation of this algorithm, new nodes are added to the left side of the OPEN list. Therefore, this list is a type of data structure called a stack.

glossary.definition: Depth-Limited Search is a modification of Depth-First Search that overcomes a drawback of Depth-First Search related to going along indefinitely deep paths. It defines a depth limit that restricts how deeply the state space graph can be explored. The algorithm checks if the current node is the search goal node and, if not, checks if its depth is lower than the specified depth limit. If the depth limit is reached, it stops further exploration.
glossary.sentence: Depth-Limited Search is a modification of Depth-First Search that overcomes a drawback of Depth-First Search related to going along indefinitely deep paths. It defines a depth limit that restricts how deeply the state space graph can be explored. The algorithm checks if the current node is the search goal node and, if not, checks if its depth is lower than the specified depth limit. If the depth limit is reached, it stops further exploration.

glossary.definition: A depth limit is a parameter that defines how deeply the state space graph can be explored by the algorithm. The algorithm checks if the current node's depth is lower than this limit before proceeding further exploration. In the example, the depth limit is set to 2.
glossary.sentence: A depth limit is a parameter that defines how deeply the state space graph can be explored by the algorithm. The algorithm checks if the current node's depth is lower than this limit before proceeding further exploration. In the example, the depth limit is set to 2.

glossary.definition: An iteration refers to one complete cycle of processing in an algorithm. In the example, each iteration corresponds to a step where the algorithm expands a node and processes its descendants.
glossary.sentence: An iteration refers to one complete cycle of processing in an algorithm. In the example, each iteration corresponds to a step where the algorithm expands a node and processes its descendants.

glossary.definition: An ancestor is a node that is on the path leading up to the current node in the state space graph. For instance, in the example, the node D is an ancestor of node J.
glossary.sentence: An ancestor is a node that is on the path leading up to the current node in the state space graph. For instance, in the example, the node D is an ancestor of node J.

glossary.definition: A descendant is a node that is on the path leading from the current node in the state space graph. For instance, in the example, the node J is a descendant of node D.
glossary.sentence: A descendant is a node that is on the path leading from the current node in the state space graph. For instance, in the example, the node J is a descendant of node D.

glossary.definition: The algorithm starts simultaneously from the search start node and the search goal node. It explores closer branches of both nodes until it finds a point of contact.
glossary.sentence: Bidirectional Search starts simultaneously from the search start node and the search goal node. It explores closer branches of both nodes until it finds a point of contact.

glossary.definition: The domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.
glossary.sentence: The domain expert has domain-specific heuristic knowledge that allows him to solve problems in a more effective way.

glossary.definition: A heuristic evaluation function is a function that estimates the value of a game state based on certain heuristics. It is used to evaluate the potential moves and determine which move leads to the best possible outcome for the current player.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic evaluation function is a function that estimates the value of a game state based on certain heuristics.

glossary.definition: An algorithm that always moves to the best node in the state space graph, considering the heuristic measures of nodes as a mountain where the search goal is the top and the start node is the bottom.
glossary.sentence: The idea of the Hill Climbing algorithm is to always move to the best node in the state space graph. If we imagine that the state space graph with heuristic measures of nodes is similar to a mountain, then the search goal node can be considered to be the top of the mountain, while the search start node is the foot of the mountain (Figure 4).

glossary.definition: Heuristic measures are values assigned to nodes in the state space graph to evaluate their quality and determine which moves are promising during game play.
glossary.sentence: Heuristic measures are given close to nodes, and a smaller heuristic measure represents a better quality of node. ... The heuristic evaluation function, which gives heuristic measures of 2, 0, 1, and 3, respectively.

glossary.definition: The algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction )."  , "sentence": "The algorithm does not reach the search goal node because it finds a node that has a better heuristic measure than the heuristic measures of its descendants/ancestors (depending on the search direction ); the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node."  , "language": "en"}, {
glossary.sentence: the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: The algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: the plateau problem : the algorithm cannot continue the search if the heuristic measure of its best descendant/ancestor (depending on the search direction) is equal to the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: The Best-First Search algorithm does not have these shortcomings. Let’s consider it. In each iteration, Best-First Search selects the best node found so far, but it also keeps all of the nodes that were found before.
glossary.sentence: The Best-First Search algorithm does not have these shortcomings. Let’s consider it. In each iteration, Best-First Search selects the best node found so far, but it also keeps all of the nodes that were found before.

glossary.definition: The beam width is the number of nodes that are considered in each iteration of the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: Other nodes are placed on the CLOSED list. Therefore, the OPEN list always contains the same number of nodes as the beam width, excluding: iteration 0, when only the search start node is added to the OPEN list, and a case when the number of direct descendants/ancestors is less than the beam width.

glossary.definition: Hill Climbing will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is higher than the heuristic measure of the current node, and vice versa—if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the smallest measure, the algorithm will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is less than the heuristic measure of the current node.
glossary.sentence: Hill Climbing will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is higher than the heuristic measure of the current node, and vice versa—if the function defines the best heuristic measure to be the smallest measure, the algorithm will always choose a node whose heuristic measure is less than the heuristic measure of the current node.

glossary.definition: A numerical value assigned to a game state to represent the outcome for the player, which can be precise (known outcome) or intermediate (estimated range based on known values).
glossary.sentence: Step 1: The algorithm assigns a precise heuristic measure of "1" to the leaf node, since this is a state where the maximiser wins the game, because this player has the highest score.

glossary.definition: A partial state space graph is a sub-graph of the complete state space graph, and usually includes states between a specific node and a predefined depth.
glossary.sentence: A partial state space graph is a sub-graph of the complete state space graph, and usually includes states between a specific node and a predefined depth.

glossary.definition: A complete state space graph includes all states, from the initial state of the game (represented by the root node of the graph), to all goal states of the game (represented by leaf nodes).
glossary.sentence: The complete state space graph includes all states, from the initial state of the game (represented by the root node of the graph), to all goal states of the game (represented by leaf nodes).

glossary.definition: A maximiser is a player who aims to win the game by trying to lead the game into a state that is the worst outcome for the minimiser.
glossary.sentence: Labeling players of the game In both cases, the players of the game are labelled as: a maximiser – where the strategy of this player is to win the game; a minimiser – where this player's strategy is to do everything possible to prevent the maximiser from winning the game, by trying to lead the game into a state that is the worst outcome for the maximiser.

glossary.definition: A minimiser is a player who aims to prevent the maximiser from winning the game by trying to lead the game into a state that is the worst outcome for the maximiser.
glossary.sentence: Labeling players of the game In both cases, the players of the game are labelled as: a maximiser – where the strategy of this player is to win the game; a minimiser – where this player's strategy is to do everything possible to prevent the maximiser from winning the game, by trying to lead the game into a state that is the worst outcome for the maximiser.

glossary.definition: Game development refers to the process of creating and developing games, including designing the game mechanics, characters, levels, etc., as well as implementing them using various technologies and tools.
glossary.sentence: To implement a game, it is not sufficient to represent the game using a state space graph. We also need to equip an intelligent computer system with an algorithm that will process the graph, with the aim of imitating game playing and striving to win the game (Figure 1).

glossary.definition: A search-based game algorithm is an algorithm used in game development to find solutions by exploring the state space graph through a series of searches.
glossary.sentence: Application of a search-based game algorithm In the case of a complete state space graph, a search-based game algorithm can be applied to the whole graph; in this case, root nodes and leaf nodes are clearly represented, and it is possible to identify a game result at each of the leaf nodes (Figure 2). In the case of a partial state space graph, a search-based game algorithm is applied to only a sub-graph of the complete search state space graph. As a result, the root and leaf nodes are mostly unavailable.

glossary.definition: Terminal nodes are nodes in a state space graph that represent the end of a game, such as winning or losing conditions.
glossary.sentence: In the case of a partial state space graph, a search-based game algorithm is applied to only a sub-graph of the complete search state space graph. As a result, the root and leaf nodes are mostly unavailable. Instead of these nodes, the algorithm processes (Figure 3): a node that represents the current state of the game; and the terminal nodes of the sub-graph at the specified depth, even though it is not possible to identify a game result at any of the terminal nodes.

glossary.definition: A level in a state space graph represents a layer or a stage in the game, where each level corresponds to a player's turn, with moves being made alternately by the maximiser and minimiser.
glossary.sentence: Dividing the state space graph into levels In both cases, the available graph is alternately divided into levels representing the maximiser (labelled as MAX) and minimiser (labelled as MIN), remembering that moves in two-player games of perfect information are made alternately.

glossary.definition: A precise heuristic measure assigned to a leaf node in Alpha-beta pruning, indicating that the player has a known outcome based on the game's score.
glossary.sentence: Step 1: The algorithm goes down using the first unconsidered path on the left until it reaches a leaf node. It assigns a precise heuristic measure of "1" to the leaf node, since this is a state where the maximiser wins the game, because this player has the highest score.

glossary.definition: An intermediate heuristic measure used when a precise heuristic measure cannot be assigned due to lack of information about the game's outcome. It represents an estimated range based on known values, such as the worst-case or best-case scenario.
glossary.sentence: Step 3: The algorithm again goes up one level and finds that the node with the numerical string "2-3-3" has another unconsidered descendant. It is not possible to assign a precise heuristic measure in this case, and the algorithm is able to assign only an intermediate heuristic measure of >=1, since the node is located at MAX level and the only known heuristic measure is 1.

glossary.definition: An alpha-cut-off occurs when the current node's beta value (intermediate heuristic measure) is smaller than the ancestor's alpha value (precise heuristic measure), indicating that the unconsidered paths below the current node can be cut off to optimize the search.
glossary.sentence: Step 6: When the algorithm has assigned an intermediate heuristic measure, it checks whether the ancestor of the current node along the current path also has an intermediate heuristic measure. This check gives a positive result. The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the beta value) is -1 and the measure of the ancestor (the alpha value) is 1. From a comparison of the measures, the algorithm finds that the value of beta is smaller than alpha, meaning that an alpha-cut-off should be performed by cutting off the unconsidered paths below the current node.

glossary.definition: The level in the game tree where a node is considered to be a maximiser's turn, and thus can have a precise heuristic measure that reflects the maximum possible outcome for the player.
glossary.sentence: Step 8: The current node is located at the MAX level, and thus acquires the maximum heuristic measure from its direct descendants, i.e. a value of 1.

glossary.definition: The level in the game tree where a node is considered to be a minimiser's turn, and thus can have an intermediate heuristic measure that reflects the minimum possible outcome for the player.
glossary.sentence: Step 9: The algorithm has assigned this intermediate heuristic measure, and the current node is located at the MIN level, but there is no difference between the heuristic measures of the descendants. The current node therefore acquires a heuristic measure of 1.

glossary.definition: The precise heuristic measure assigned to an ancestor node in Alpha-beta pruning, representing the best possible outcome for the maximiser at that level.
glossary.sentence: Step 6: The measure of the ancestor (the alpha value) is 1.

glossary.definition: The intermediate heuristic measure assigned to a current node in Alpha-beta pruning, representing an estimated range for the outcome based on known values from its descendants.
glossary.sentence: Step 6: The intermediate heuristic measure of the current node (the beta value) is -1 and the measure of the ancestor (the alpha value) is 1.

glossary.definition: The minimax algorithm is a decision-making method used in game theory to determine the best possible move for a player. It assumes that the opponent will play optimally to minimize the current player's advantage. The algorithm works by recursively evaluating all possible moves and their outcomes, considering both maximizing and minimizing scenarios.
glossary.sentence: The minimax algorithm is a decision-making method used in game theory to determine the best possible move for a player.

glossary.definition: Alpha-beta pruning is an optimization technique used in the minimax algorithm to reduce the number of nodes evaluated. It works by maintaining two values, alpha and beta, which represent the range of the current node's possible outcomes. If a node's value is found to be worse than the previously known value, it can be pruned.
glossary.sentence: Alpha-beta pruning is an optimization technique used in the minimax algorithm to reduce the number of nodes evaluated.

glossary.definition: The horizon effect refers to the phenomenon where a heuristic evaluation function with limited look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, leading to suboptimal decisions in the game. This can result in the entire game being lost due to incomplete information.
glossary.sentence: Horizon effect refers to the phenomenon where a heuristic evaluation function with limited look-ahead may not detect a heuristically promising path, leading to suboptimal decisions in the game.

glossary.definition: Iterative deepening is a search strategy that explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner. It starts with a shallow depth limit and gradually increases it until a desired depth is reached. This approach ensures that all possible states are explored, even if they are not found in earlier iterations.
glossary.sentence: Iterative deepening is a search strategy that explores the partial state space graph in an iterative manner.

glossary.definition: Machine learning is a field of computer science that focuses on developing algorithms and models that can learn from data without explicit programming. It involves the use of statistical methods, probability theory, linear algebra, multivariate calculus, and optimization techniques to analyze data and find patterns.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning is a field of computer science that focuses on developing algorithms and models that can learn from data without explicit programming.

glossary.definition: Supervised learning is also called learning with a teacher. It is a type of explicit learning. Supervised learning is similar to learning letters or numbers in school: the teacher shows and pronounces a letter or a number, he/she repeats this process several times, and, in the end, children can recognise different letters and numbers.
glossary.sentence: Supervised learning - [definition]

glossary.definition: Unsupervised learning is a type of implicit learning. It is similar to learning from experience by observing patterns in the data without any explicit labels or guidance from a teacher.
glossary.sentence: Unsupervised learning - [definition]

glossary.definition: Reinforcement learning is a type of learning where an agent learns to make decisions by performing actions in an environment to achieve a goal. The agent receives rewards or penalties based on its actions, and it learns from these experiences to improve future decision-making.
glossary.sentence: Reinforcement learning - [definition]

glossary.definition: Conventional algorithms are explicitly programmed by their developers. They are sets of exact and complete instructions that fulfill a specific task, providing output only for known inputs. These algorithms can make errors when given unknown inputs and are not practical for complex problems with incomplete information.
glossary.sentence: Conventional algorithms are explicitly programmed by their developers.

glossary.definition: Machine learning algorithms are not based on a precise set of instructions. Instead, they rely on general guidelines and initial configuration variables (hyperparameters) to build a mathematical model that learns from data.
glossary.sentence: Machine learning algorithms are not based on a precise set of instructions.

glossary.definition: a particular unit of data typically represented by a number of feature values, for example, a data object can represent one specific patient through the values of patient’s temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, etc.
glossary.sentence: A data object (also called a data point, an observation, an example) - a particular unit of data typically represented by a number of feature values, for example, a data object can represent one specific patient through the values of patient’s temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, etc.

glossary.definition: a collection of data objects, for example, a set of data about patients who attended Hospital A between January and May in 2019.
glossary.sentence: A dataset - a collection of data objects, for example, a set of data about patients who attended Hospital A between January and May in 2019.

glossary.definition: In the case of Linear regression, the changing output is a value of the cost function across the whole dataset, input values are weight values of the linear function and independent variables are presented by a feature vector.
glossary.sentence: In the case of Linear regression, the changing output is a value of the cost function across the whole dataset, input values are weight values of the linear function and independent variables are presented by a feature vector.

glossary.definition: a specific value of a feature, for example, if the feature is "the patient’s temperature", then its value for a specific patient can be 38oC.
glossary.sentence: A feature value – a specific value of a feature, for example, if the feature is "the patient’s temperature", then its value for a specific patient can be 38oC.

glossary.definition: a set of all features characterising a data object, for example, the patient's temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, and others can represent one patient in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: A feature set – a set of all features characterising a data object, for example, the patient's temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, and others can represent one patient in the dataset.

glossary.definition: an external configuration variable whose value is typically set by the developer arbitrarily before the training (for example, learning rate, number of neurons in an artificial neural network, etc.).
glossary.sentence: A hyperparameter (also called a model hyperparameter) - an external configuration variable whose value is typically set by the developer arbitrarily before the training (for example, learning rate, number of neurons in an artificial neural network, etc.).

glossary.definition: a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm.
glossary.sentence: A model - a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: a result of applying a model to given data objects.
glossary.sentence: Model output - a result of applying a model to given data objects.

glossary.definition: an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.
glossary.sentence: A parameter (also called a model parameter) - an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learned) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.

glossary.definition: a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.
glossary.sentence: A test set – a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model.

glossary.definition: a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.
glossary.sentence: A training set - a subset of the dataset used in the training of the model.

glossary.definition: For univariate linear regression, there is a unique solution because the weights of the model can be calculated using the following equations (Russell and Norvig, 2010): ... The gradient descent technique thus allows the cost function value to be minimised according to changes in the values of the weights of the linear function.
glossary.sentence: For univariate linear regression, there is a unique solution because the weights of the model can be calculated using the following equations (Russell and Norvig, 2010): However, calculating the weights for the case of multiple input features is not a trivial task.

glossary.definition: The gradient descent is an optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values. The gradient descent is based on calculating a partial derivative with respect to all independent variables. In the case of Linear regression, the changing output is a value of the cost function across the whole dataset, input values are weight values of the linear function and independent variables are presented by a feature vector.
glossary.sentence: The gradient descent technique thus allows the cost function value to be minimised according to changes in the values of the weights of the linear function. Actually, you do not need to know the calculus underlying the gradient descent in detail because the tools, frameworks and languages that exist for using linear regression will automatically handle these computations.

glossary.definition: There is also a closed-form solution like this one: However, this solution is not used in practice because the computation of matrices can fail if there is a problem of matrix singularity.
glossary.sentence: However, this solution is not used in practice because the computation of matrices can fail if there is a problem of matrix singularity.

glossary.definition: Additionally, calculating the loss function for every possible value of each weight over the entire dataset would be an inefficient way of finding the convergence point (Google Developers, n.d.).
glossary.sentence: Additionally, calculating the loss function for every possible value of each weight over the entire dataset would be an inefficient way of finding the convergence point.

glossary.definition: The gradient descent is an optimisation technique used to measure changes in the output regarding changes in input values. The gradient descent is based on calculating a partial derivative with respect to all independent variables. In the case of Linear regression, the changing output is a value of the cost function across the whole dataset, input values are weight values of the linear function and independent variables are presented by a feature vector.
glossary.sentence: The gradient descent technique thus allows the cost function value to be minimised according to changes in the values of the weights of the linear function. Actually, you do not need to know the calculus underlying the gradient descent in detail because the tools, frameworks and languages that exist for using linear regression will automatically handle these computations.

glossary.definition: On the one hand, if the learning rate is low, the algorithm will need to perform many iterations before minimising the error. So, the process of building a mathematical model will be slow and computationally intensive. But, on the other hand, if the learning rate is too high, the algorithm can miss the error minima. Usually, finding an acceptable learning rate is an empirical process based on experimenting with the different learning rate values.
glossary.sentence: Usually, the learning rate is associated with the algorithm’s speed of learning the underlying mathematical model or, in other words, how fast the algorithm converges to the error minima.

glossary.definition: Logistic regression has the following cost function , which should also be minimised: ... The simplified steps of Logistic regression are shown in Figure 10.
glossary.sentence: Logistic regression has the following cost function , which should also be minimised: ...

glossary.definition: Previously you have learnt that supervised machine learning is used for regression and classification tasks. Linear regression is intended for the regression task while logistic regression – for the classification task. k-Nearest Neighbour (kNN) algorithm Previously you have learnt that supervised machine learning is used for regression and classification tasks.
glossary.sentence: Previously you have learnt that supervised machine learning is used for regression and classification tasks. Linear regression is intended for the regression task while logistic regression – for the classification task. k-Nearest Neighbour (kNN) algorithm Previously you have learnt that supervised machine learning is used for regression and classification tasks.

glossary.definition: The classification task involves determining the class label of a new data object based on its features. The algorithm identifies the most similar neighbour in the existing dataset and assigns its category/class label to the new data object.
glossary.sentence: Assume we have a dataset that includes data objects of two classes labelled as suns and clouds. The algorithm should identify class labels for two new data objects represented in yellow. Thus, when searching for an answer to the question of what the category or class of a new data object is, the algorithm finds the most similar neighbour in the existing dataset and assigns its category/class label to the new data object.

glossary.definition: There are more than two classes/categories in multiclass classification ; for example, in animal classification, we can use labels like Cat, Dog, Horse, etc. ; we can classify movies according to their genres like Romance, Comedy, Horror, Drama, etc.
glossary.sentence: There are more than two classes/categories in multiclass classification ; for example, in animal classification, we can use labels like Cat, Dog, Horse, etc. ; we can classify movies according to their genres like Romance, Comedy, Horror, Drama, etc.

glossary.definition: In the simplest case of classification, it is the so-called binary classification when data are classified in two classes or, in other words, the output variable can take either of two possible values, for example, whether an e-mail is a spam or not, a review is a positive or negative, a student has passed or failed the study course, etc.
glossary.sentence: In the simplest case of classification, it is the so-called binary classification when data are classified in two classes or, in other words, the output variable can take either of two possible values...

glossary.definition: Supervised machine learning is used for a regression and a classification task. Previously you have learnt that linear regression is suitable for the regression task while logistic regression – for the classification task.
glossary.sentence: Supervised machine learning is used for a regression and a classification task.

glossary.definition: Linear regression is intended for the regression task while logistic regression – for the classification task. The algorithm's performance is evaluated using the mean square error that accumulates the differences in the distance between the predicted output value and the real output value.
glossary.sentence: Linear regression is intended for the regression task while logistic regression – for the classification task.

glossary.definition: A feature vector of a data object is a collection of features that describe the object. The values of the features are used to calculate the distance between two data objects.
glossary.sentence: The features “Average mark in the semester work” and “The number of missed lectures” have continuous values, therefore the Euclidean distance is calculated. In turn, the features that represent the student's evaluations in the laboratory works have categorical values, so the Hamming distance is used to determine whether the feature values of the data objects are the same or different.

glossary.definition: The sigmoid function is represented in the following way: The graphical representation of the sigmoid function is given in Figure 9. It allows understanding of why the threshold value of 0.5 at which the function crosses the y axis is used to decide about a class/category of a data object.
glossary.sentence: The sigmoid function is represented in the following way: ... It allows understanding of why the threshold value of 0.5 at which the function crosses the y axis is used to decide about a class/category of a data object.

glossary.definition: The threshold value of 0.5 at which the function crosses the y axis is used to decide about a class/category of a data object.
glossary.sentence: The threshold value of 0.5 at which the function crosses the y axis is used to decide about a class/category of a data object.

glossary.definition: The kNN algorithm is a machine learning method that uses the distance between data objects to make predictions. It works by calculating the distance between a new data object and the current data objects in the dataset, sorting them by distance, selecting the top k nearest neighbors, and using their labels to predict the output.
glossary.sentence: The kNN algorithm is simple enough and does not have many hyperparameters that should be tuned. Moreover, from the steps given above, one can conclude that this algorithm does not have a separate training phase.

glossary.definition: The dataset used in the logistic regression is similar to the data set shown in Table 3, with one exception: labels of data objects are represented as classes. For example, we have a dataset about students' average marks in the semester work and the number of missed lectures (Table 5). In addition, the dataset contains labels showing if a specific student passed or failed the study course in the past.
glossary.sentence: The dataset used in the logistic regression is similar to the data set shown in Table 3, with one exception: labels of data objects are represented as classes.

glossary.definition: The algorithm this value is greater than or equal to the threshold value. If this is the case, then the data object is assigned a class label of
glossary.sentence: If this is the case, then the data object is assigned a class label of

glossary.definition: The nearest neighbours are identified for two new data objects based on distance and one data object receives a label of sun, another – of cloud.
glossary.sentence: The nearest neighbours are identified for two new data objects based on distance and one data object receives a label of sun, another – of cloud.

glossary.definition: The concept of similarity (sometimes called distance, proximity, or closeness) makes the basis for the algorithm. In this case, the similarity of data objects is determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects (the so-called feature similarity).
glossary.sentence: The concept of similarity (sometimes called distance, proximity, or closeness) makes the basis for the algorithm. In this case, the similarity of data objects is determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects (the so-called feature similarity).

glossary.definition: The Euclidean distance measures the square root of the sum of the squares of the differences between the value of the i- th feature of a new data object and the value of the i- th feature of an existing data object in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: The Euclidean distance measures the square root of the sum of the squares of the differences between the value of the i- th feature of a new data object and the value of the i- th feature of an existing data object in the dataset.

glossary.definition: The Manhattan distance calculates the sum of absolute differences between feature values.
glossary.sentence: The Manhattan distance calculates the sum of absolute differences between feature values.

glossary.definition: The Hamming distance is used for categorical feature values that calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ. If the values of the same feature of both data objects are the same, then the distance is equal to 0; otherwise, it is equal to 1.
glossary.sentence: If the values of the same feature of both data objects are the same, then the distance is equal to 0; otherwise, it is equal to 1.

glossary.definition: The similarity of data objects is determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects (the so-called feature similarity).
glossary.sentence: The similarity of data objects is determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects (the so-called feature similarity).

glossary.definition: Any distance metric must satisfy the following requirements: 1. the distance must never be negative; 2. the distance between two identical feature vectors is zero; 3. the distance from x to y is the same as the distance from y to x ; 4. the metric must satisfy the triangular inequality.
glossary.sentence: Any distance metric must satisfy the following requirements: 1. the distance must never be negative; 2. the distance between two identical feature vectors is zero; 3. the distance from x to y is the same as the distance from y to x ; 4. the metric must satisfy the triangular inequality.

glossary.definition: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object. The developer sets a value of the hyperparameter before the operation of the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: In the kNN version of the algorithm, a hyperparameter k is used. It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object. The developer sets a value of the hyperparameter before the operation of the algorithm.

glossary.definition: In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closest data object in the dataset. However, deciding the output value for a new data object based only on one closest data object leads to unstable decision boundaries.
glossary.sentence: In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closest data object in the dataset. However, deciding the output value for a new data object based only on one closest data object leads to unstable decision boundaries.

glossary.definition: The hyperparameter k defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object. The developer sets a value of the hyperparameter before the operation of the algorithm.
glossary.sentence: The developer sets a value of the hyperparameter k before the operation of the algorithm.

glossary.definition: The algorithm applies a voting mechanism; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification).
glossary.sentence: The algorithm applies a voting mechanism; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification).

glossary.definition: The majority vote in the case of binary classification.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification).

glossary.definition: The metric must satisfy the triangular inequality: In general, we can differentiate two versions of the algorithm. In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closest data object in the dataset. However, deciding the output value for a new data object based only on one closest data object leads to unstable decision boundaries.
glossary.sentence: The metric must satisfy the triangular inequality: In general, we can differentiate two versions of the algorithm. In the 1NN version (one nearest neighbour), the algorithm classifies a new data object by finding a single, but the closest data object in the dataset. However, deciding the output value for a new data object based only on one closest data object leads to unstable decision boundaries.

glossary.definition: The distance from x to y is the same as the distance from y to x.
glossary.sentence: The distance from x to y is the same as the distance from y to x ;

glossary.definition: Datasets include mixed-type data – both continuous and categorical. To cope with these data, we can rely on the sum of the squared distances along corresponding features.
glossary.sentence: As quite usually datasets include mixed-type data – both continuous and categorical, to cope with these data, we can rely on the sum of the squared distances along corresponding features (Kubat, 2017): Figure 14 demonstrates the calculation of the distance for mixed-type feature values.

glossary.definition: The sum of the squared distances along corresponding features is used to cope with mixed-type data.
glossary.sentence: The sum of the squared distances along corresponding features is used to cope with mixed-type data.

glossary.definition: The Silhouette Score is a measure of how well clusters are formed in a dataset. It ranges from -1 to 1, where 1 indicates that the cluster is well-separated and adjacent to other clusters, while -1 indicates that the cluster is poorly separated or overlapping with another cluster.
glossary.sentence: The size of the silhouettes is proportional to the number of data objects inside a specific cluster. Information sources Chapelle, O., Scholkopf, B., & Zien, A. (2006). Semi-Supervised Learning. The MIT Press. Geron A. (2017). Hands-On Machine Learning with Scikit-Learn and TensorFlow: Concepts, Tools, and Techniques to Build Intelligent Systems. O’Reilly Media, Inc. Kubat, M. (2017). An Introduction to Machine Learning. Springer International Publishing. Russell, S., & Norvig P. (2010). Artificial Intelligence: A Modern Approach. Topic 4.5 - Basics of Artificial Neural Networks Nowadays, artificial neural networks are experiencing their heyday. Few of us have not heard of the concept of deep learning, which is closely related to artificial neural networks. Artificial neural networks have found application in various fields: text classification and categorization, student performance modelling, character recognition, credit card attrition, video surveillance, stock market prediction, cancer cell analysis, vehicle scheduling, etc. Moreover, artificial neural networks can be used for carrying out both supervised and unsupervised machine learning tasks.

glossary.definition: The Silhouette Plot is a visualization tool that displays the cluster labels on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis. It helps in assessing the quality of clustering by showing how well each data point belongs to its cluster.
glossary.sentence: The Silhouette Plot , representing the cluster label on the y-axis and the Silhouette Score on the x-axis (Fig.6). The size of the silhouettes is proportional to the number of data objects inside a specific cluster.

glossary.definition: An artificial neuron is a simplified model of a biological neuron, designed to mimic its structure and function. It receives input signals, processes them using weights and an activation function, and produces an output signal.
glossary.sentence: Artificial neuron In 1943, McCulloch and Pitts defined a mathematical model of the neuron (Ertel, 2011; Jones, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011), which laid fundamentals for artificial neural networks. In general, an artificial neuron has (Callan, 2003; Hopgood, 2001; Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011): one or more inputs (sometimes also called input signals) which are represented by numerical values that enter from the environment or are delivered by other neurons. The numerical values from the environment typically correspond to the values of data object features in the dataset. The inputs typically are labelled by indexed x; a set of real-valued weights (one for each input) that express the strength, or, in other words, the importance of each neuron input in terms of its influence on the output. Weights are the basic means of long-term memory in artificial neural networks. They are determined in the process of neural network learning. Weights are typically represented by indexed w; an output that is represented by a numerical value and can be either a final solution of the artificial neural network or output to other neurons. It is usually represented by indexed y; a function for combining input signals and providing a single numerical value. It is typically a summation function that sums up the weighted values of inputs. However, other functions are also allowed; a transfer (activation) function that determines the output of the neuron and allows the neural network to model complex non-linear relationships; a bias input which is also commonly applied, and it provides the offset of the line/hyperplane from the origin. If no bias existed, the line (or the hyperplane) would be restricted to pass through the origin, and the weights would only control the angle of the discriminant. The bias is commonly set to one, but the learning algorithm of an artificial neural network should tune its weight. The bias input is typically indexed with 0 index.

glossary.definition: A biological neuron is a type of nerve cell found in the human brain. It has a cell body (soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions: dendrites and axons. Dendrites receive signals from other neurons, while the axon transmits signals to synapses.
glossary.sentence: The human brain consists of cells called neurons. Each neuron has a cell body (a soma) containing a nucleus and two types of root-like extensions from the cell body: many dendrites and a single axon (Fig. Dendrites look like a little brush and serve for receiving signals from other neurons. The axon itself eventually splits into a number of branches, each terminating in a little endbulb that comes close to the dendrites of another neuron. A sending neuron transmits a signal along its axon to a synapse, which is the site where the end of an axon branch comes close to a dendrite or the cell body of another neuron. When the signal from the sending neuron reaches the synapse, it generates an electrical signal in the dendrites of the receiving neuron. Thus, the primary activity of a neuron is to fire an electrical impulse along its axon. The single most important fact about the firing of neurons is that it depends upon the activity at the synapses. Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively. If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight, then we can calculate the strength of each synapse (by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight). Adding all the synapses together gives the total strength of the signals received at the synapses – and hence the total input to the neuron. If this total input exceeds the threshold of the neuron, then the neuron will fire (the fragment is adopted from (Bermúdez, 2014)). Therefore, the human mental activity consists primarily of electrochemical activity in networks of neurons (Russell & Norvig, 2010). According to (Negnevitsky , 2011), the human brain is a highly complex, non-linear and parallel processing system that processes information simultaneously throughout the whole network rather than at a specific location. Learning in human neural networks is associated with synapses. It is known that a synapse is made stronger by the however much more electrical current it must carry. Stronger here means that the synapse has a higher conductivity. Synapses that are used often obtain an increasingly higher weight. For synapses that are used infrequently or are not active at all, the conductivity continues to decrease. This can even lead to them dying off (the fragment is adopted from (Ertel, 2011)).

glossary.definition: A synapse is a junction between two neurons or a neuron and an external entity. It allows for the transmission of signals between neurons or between a neuron and an external entity. Synapses are essential in neural communication, as they facilitate the transfer of information through electrical impulses.
glossary.sentence: The single most important fact about the firing of neurons is that it depends upon the activity at the synapses. Some of the signals reaching the neuron's dendrites promote firing, and others inhibit it. These are called excitatory and inhibitory synapses, respectively. If we think of an excitatory synapse as having a positive weight and an inhibitory synapse a negative weight, then we can calculate the strength of each synapse (by multiplying the strength of the incoming signal by the corresponding synaptic weight). Adding all the synapses together gives the total strength of the signals received at the synapses – and hence the total input to the neuron. If this total input exceeds the threshold of the neuron, then the neuron will fire (the fragment is adopted from (Bermúdez, 2014)). Therefore, the human mental activity consists primarily of electrochemical activity in networks of neurons (Russell & Norvig, 2010). According to (Negnevitsky , 2011), the human brain is a highly complex, non-linear and parallel processing system that processes information simultaneously throughout the whole network rather than at a specific location. Learning in human neural networks is associated with synapses. It is known that a synapse is made stronger by the however much more electrical current it must carry. Stronger here means that the synapse has a higher conductivity. Synapses that are used often obtain an increasingly higher weight. For synapses that are used infrequently or are not active at all, the conductivity continues to decrease. This can even lead to them dying off (the fragment is adopted from (Ertel, 2011)).

glossary.definition: The knowledge base includes IF... rules (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011). It contains the rules that are used for reasoning.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge base includes IF... rules (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base. However, the knowledge engineer is not a specialist in all problem domains.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge engineer is a specialist who knows well methods and tools of artificial intelligence, as well as who has knowledge how to describe and represent knowledge in the knowledge base. However, the knowledge engineer is not a specialist in all problem domains.

glossary.definition: An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts.
glossary.sentence: An expert system is a knowledge-based intelligent system that solves problems in a narrowly specialised domain in a way and at a performance level similar to one of the human experts.

glossary.definition: A knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base. Such systems can offer a variety of user interfaces, for example, they could offer creation of diagrams representing some scenarios from the problem domain or the creation of structured description of knowledge.
glossary.sentence: The knowledge acquisition system is an automated system that could be used either by the domain expert directly or by the knowledge engineer to describe knowledge and to build a knowledge base. Such systems can offer a variety of user interfaces, for example, they could offer creation of diagrams representing some scenarios from the problem domain or the creation of structured description of knowledge.

glossary.definition: Networked knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a directed labelled graph, where each node displays a simple object/concept and directed labelled arcs represent relations between them.
glossary.sentence: Networked knowledge representation schemas are based on associationist theories of human mind that were developed in psychology. These theories postulate that knowledge on different concepts is related by associations in the human mind and these associations define the meaning of a concept in relation to meaning of other concepts (Luger, 2009), for example, the concept 'university' for the most part of students could be associated with such concepts as lectures, students, professors, exams, science etc. All the mentioned concepts define the meaning of the concept 'university'.

glossary.definition: Logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Logical knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of logical statements, using constants, variables, functions, predicates, logical operators, and apply inference rules that allow getting new true knowledge from a set of true knowledge.
