glossary.definition: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/concept.
glossary.sentence: Structured knowledge representation schemas also use a graph to represent knowledge, but each node is displayed as a complex structure with all characteristics and components of the object/concept.

glossary.term: procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schema
glossary.definition: Procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.
glossary.sentence: Procedural or rule-based knowledge representation schemas represent knowledge in the form of a set of procedures/instructions.

glossary.definition: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain (Negnevitsky, 2011).
glossary.sentence: Anyone can be considered a domain expert if he/she has deep knowledge (of both facts and rules) and substantial practical experience in a particular (usually limited) domain (Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A data-structure for representing a stereotyped situation (Minsky, 1975). It is a data structure that represents typical knowledge about an object/concept (Callan, 2003; Negnevitsky , 2011) or, in other words, information relevant to stereotyped entities (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: The frame has a specific structure (Fig.2) that includes: slot titles, slot values and facets ( Negnevitsky , 2011). Each frame has a unique title or identifier in the knowledge base.

glossary.definition: A property of objects/concepts (Jones, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992). It can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or contain a title of another frame.
glossary.sentence: Each property has a value that can be represented as a symbolic, numerical or boolean value, as well as it can be empty or can contain a title of another frame.

glossary.definition: An extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value ( Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: Facets can contain value type, value constraints (maximum value, minimum value, range of values, etc.), and actions that can be performed with slot values.

glossary.definition: A class/generic frame. It contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts. The class frames help us to represent a problem in an abstract form ( Negnevitsky , 2011). Typically, a class frame includes a title of the class/set/type/kind, a set of common properties, and values of properties that are common to all objects of the class/set/type/kind.
glossary.sentence: Traditionally slot values in the class frame are empty, or they contain default values. Using this type of frames, we can also create sub-classes that are classes derived from other classes (Figure 5).

glossary.definition: It represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain (Figure 6). Typically the instance frames include all slot values and are derived from the class/sub-class frames. However, instance frames can also contain slots that are not defined for class/sub-class frames.
glossary.sentence: These are the frames that an intelligent computer system uses to solve problems.

glossary.definition: It contains new slots that are not included in the class frames but characterize objects belonging to the sub-class. As a result, the class frames allow creating multi-level hierarchies of class and sub-class frames.
glossary.sentence: The sub-class frames can contain new slots that are not included in the class frames but characterize objects belonging to the sub-class.

glossary.definition: It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole.
glossary.sentence: In different shells for creating frame-based knowledge bases, this type of relationship can be represented by slot titles "a-part-of" or "part-whole".

glossary.definition: It relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame. It allows making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels.
glossary.sentence: Figure 8 demonstrates an example of the generalization relation between a sub-class Man and a class Human, and an instance John and a sub-class Man.

glossary.definition: The generalization relation ensures inheritance in frames. Therefore, sub-class and instance frames have a possibility to inherit slots and their values from class frames, but they can also contain properties that are not represented in the class frame (Figure 11). Moreover, frames support overwriting of the inherited values.
glossary.sentence: Multiple inheritance when a frame inherits slots and slot values from several other frames simultaneously is allowed if slot titles of parent frames are unique.

glossary.definition: It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).
glossary.sentence: Association or semantic relation . It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).

glossary.definition: A slot can have a procedural attachment ( Negnevitsky , 2011). In general, it is possible to add regular procedures and/or demons.
glossary.sentence: A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011). We can use such procedures for different purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating some numerical values, such as the amount of taxes to be paid, acquiring data from external sources, etc.

glossary.definition: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992).
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system (Jones, 2009; Luger, 2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992).

glossary.definition: The database or working memory ensures this by storing the facts acquired from the user, conclusions derived in the reasoning process, confidence measures of conclusions, information coming from external programs, dead ends in the search process, and other information relevant to the case under consideration (Luger, 2011).
glossary.sentence: The database or working memory ensures this by storing the facts acquired from the user...

glossary.definition: The inference engine performs the control cycle 'Recognize-act' (Luger, 2009) to link the rules given in the knowledge base with the facts provided in the working memory (Negnevitsky, 2011). Thus, the inference engine carries out the reasoning by interpreting and applying the rules whereby the rule-based expert system reaches a solution (Hopgood, 2001; Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: The inference engine performs the control cycle 'Recognize-act'...

glossary.definition: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system because an expert system provides the user with a recommendation, plan, advice, conclusion, or any other result. However, to accept the system’s recommendation, the human expert should be aware of how a particular conclusion was reached, what was the line of reasoning and whether it is valid or not (Luger, 2009; Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: Explanation facilities are essential for any expert system...

glossary.definition: The user interacts with the system through a user interface designed as a question-and-answer, menu-driven, natural language, or graphical interface (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: The user interacts with the system through a user interface...

glossary.definition: The working memory contains a description of the current state of the problem-solving process (Callan, 2003; Luger, 2009) that is called a pattern . The case-specific data are separated from the knowledge base (Luger, 2009).
glossary.sentence: The working memory contains a description...

glossary.definition: These data are assumed to be correct when they are used (Hopgood, 2001).
glossary.sentence: These data are assumed to be correct when they are used...

glossary.definition: If several rules match the pattern, a conflict set is acquired. Production rules in the conflict set are called to be enabled.
glossary.sentence: If several rules match the pattern, a conflict set is acquired...

glossary.definition: The selection of one rule from the conflict set is called conflict resolution . For this purpose, a conflict resolution strategy is used; one of the enabled rules is fired, and it changes the content of the working memory because its IF or THEN part is added to the working memory (depending on the search direction ); the control cycle repeats with the modified working memory; searching terminates when there are no more rules that match the content of the working memory.
glossary.sentence: The selection of one rule from the conflict set...

glossary.definition: If a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred, choose the forward chaining inference engine.
glossary.sentence: the text fragment is based on the information given in (Negnevitsky , 2011) }, {
